questions.question: Explain the two primary uses of the `super` keyword in Java constructor chaining.
keyPoints: Using `super()` to explicitly call the immediate parent class constructor.
It must be the first statement in the subclass constructor.
Using `super(argument_list)` to call a parameterized constructor of the parent class.

questions.question: Describe how the `super` keyword is used to access overridden methods and hidden members of a parent class. Provide a brief code snippet to illustrate one use case.
keyPoints: `super` provides a way to access methods and fields from the immediate parent class that have been overridden or hidden in the subclass.
It prevents infinite recursion when a subclass method overrides a parent method but still needs to use the parent's implementation.
Example: `super.display()` inside an overridden `display()` method calls the parent's version.

questions.question: A common error is a implicit super constructor is undefined error. Explain the cause of this error, the two scenarios in which it occurs, and how to resolve it. Discuss the role of the `super` keyword in the solution.
keyPoints: Cause: A subclass constructor tries to implicitly call a no-argument constructor `super()` in the parent class, but the parent class does not have one.
Scenario 1: Parent class has defined one or more parameterized constructors but no explicit default constructor.
Scenario 2: The only constructor in the parent class is parameterized.
Resolution 1: Add a no-argument constructor to the parent class.
Resolution 2: Explicitly use `super(arguments)` as the first line in the subclass constructor to call an existing parameterized parent constructor.
